lens.included_activities: US lower-middle-market firms whose primary business is preparing and filing tax returns for external individual or small-business customers, including related return review and client explanation within the engagement.
lens.excluded_activities: CPA offices, bookkeeping, payroll, billing, captive tax departments, tax software sold without an accountable service operator, volunteer preparation, non-control financing vehicles, and firms outside the roughly $1-10M normalized EBITDA band.
lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Annual or repeat outsourced tax-return engagements, generally priced per return and by form or complexity, with some add-on representation or advisory fees; revenue and staffing are highly concentrated in filing season.

a.bridge: BLS measures the broader NAICS 541200 occupation mix, not six-digit 541213, while O*NET measures the Tax Preparers occupation across employers. I used the broader mix to identify wage-bearing professional and clerical roles, mapped O*NET tasks to document intake, calculation, record review, research, drafting, client communication, and accountable judgment, then bounded the not-yet-automated wage share qualitatively. Broader-industry bookkeeping and payroll roles may bias exposure upward or downward relative to pure-play tax firms; failure to observe automation already embedded in tax software most likely biases the estimate upward.
a.rationale: The broader industry employs substantial professional and clerical labor: accountants and auditors were 31.73% of employment, tax preparers 7.13%, bookkeeping clerks 8.88%, and several administrative categories added further workflow labor (S2). O*NET describes core tax-preparer work as computing tax, completing forms, finding deductions, reviewing records, verifying entries, researching rules, interviewing clients, and explaining law (S3). Intake, extraction, classification, first-pass calculation, research, drafting, and exception flagging are exposed; client fact-finding, atypical judgment, final review, and responsibility remain less substitutable. This supports a base exposed wage share modestly above one-half.
a.change_evidence: A 541213-specific payroll-weighted staffing file joined to time-and-motion data by task, plus firm system logs separating already automated tax-software steps from human minutes, would materially change this range.
caveats: BLS publishes the cited staffing pattern at NAICS 541200, which also includes CPA, bookkeeping, and payroll businesses.
O*NET task importance is not time share, wage share, or AI substitutability.
TaxCalcBench finds frontier models completed fewer than one-third of simplified federal returns correctly, so exposure should not be read as safe autonomous execution (S4).

rho.bridge: The surveys measure current GenAI use, attitudes, and expected integration among tax/accounting professionals rather than the five-year implemented fraction of the exposed opportunity in LMM pure-play tax firms. I translate adoption momentum into workflow implementation, then discount for data security, integration, training, quality control, seasonal change capacity, and mandatory human review. Vendor-sponsored and technology-engaged respondents may bias implementation upward, while stand-alone-model benchmarks may bias it downward relative to governed tools embedded in tax software.
rho.rationale: Enterprise GenAI adoption at tax firms rose from 8% in 2024 to 21% in 2025; 79% expected significant integration by 2027, and return preparation was already a cited use case for 63% of tax/accounting GenAI users (S5). A separate survey of 700 US accounting professionals found 46% used AI daily and 81% of adopters reported productivity gains (S6). Implementation will nevertheless lag exposure because current frontier models made frequent tax-table, calculation, and eligibility errors on a simplified benchmark (S4). A five-year base near three-fifths reflects fast adoption with retained review and control bottlenecks.
rho.change_evidence: Obtain workflow-level before/after studies from 20-30 independent 541213 firms showing automated minutes, exception rates, reviewer rework, data incidents, and production use by return type over two filing seasons.
caveats: The surveys cover broader tax and accounting populations, including large firms and non-US respondents in the Thomson Reuters sample.
Reported use or perceived productivity does not establish avoided hiring or sustained labor removal.
Five-year implementation depends on integration with deterministic tax engines and secure client-data systems, not general-purpose model capability alone.

e.rationale: NAICS 541213 is already narrowly defined around non-CPA establishments providing tax-return preparation without bookkeeping, billing, or payroll, so most LMM firms in the frozen count should fit the outsourced-service lens (S1). Accounting and tax practices, including independent firms and occasional franchise units, are visibly saleable businesses (S8). The discount excludes businesses whose economics or customer book are inseparable from an owner, franchise arrangements without transferable control, poor-quality or noncompliant books, and other operational defects.
e.change_evidence: Review the frozen 40-firm universe record by record for revenue concentration, owner dependence, franchise transfer restrictions, credential coverage, compliance history, normalized EBITDA quality, and buyer-control feasibility.
caveats: No source measures eligibility among the specific frozen LMM firm universe.
The frozen n estimate is margin-bridged from size-class data and may misclassify firms whose normalized EBITDA differs from the assumed industry margin.
An establishment can fit the NAICS definition yet fail buyer diligence or lack transferable goodwill.

s5.bridge: Gallup measures stated five-year plans across US employer-business owners, not completed qualifying transfers by 541213 LMM firms. I use the 22% employer intention rate as the center, raise uncertainty for the tax/accounting market's demonstrated liquidity and owner demographics, and exclude closures, gifts without control continuity, internal reorganizations, and failed processes. Intentions can overstate completions, while unplanned and sponsor-initiated sales can make them understate actual transfers.
s5.rationale: Census data cited by Gallup show 52.3% of US employer businesses are owned by people age 55 or older, and 22% of employer-business owners said they planned to sell or transfer ownership within five years (S7). BizBuySell reports actual accounting and tax practice sales, a 2025 median sale price of $500,000, and five-year reported sale multiples, confirming an active transfer channel even though its businesses are generally smaller than the lens (S8). The base therefore stays close to the broad employer intention rate rather than treating owner age as an automatic sale.
s5.change_evidence: Acquire five years of closed 541213 transactions and failed listings from BizBuySell, Axial, Accounting Practice Sales, Poe Group Advisors, and SBA 7(a) change-of-ownership loans, matched to an estimated LMM-firm denominator.
caveats: Stated plans are not realized transfers and the Gallup population spans all industries and business sizes.
BizBuySell has no complete industry denominator and its reported accounting/tax practices often fall below the target EBITDA band.
Retirement can lead to closure or client-book runoff rather than a qualifying control transfer.

q.rationale: Per-return and form/complexity pricing weakens any automatic link between hours and customer price: H&R Block quotes an upfront base price and additional state or form-related charges (S10). Its fiscal 2025 US assisted revenue rose 6.1% as net average charge rose 5.1% and company-owned volume rose 1.0%, while total assisted volume including franchises fell 0.9%, evidence that at least a large operator can sustain price per return (S9). An acquirer can initially retain much of a labor saving, but renewals, low switching costs, DIY software, free options, and competing AI-enabled preparers should share part of the benefit over five years.
q.change_evidence: Collect anonymized engagement letters, invoices, renewal cohorts, discount histories, churn by price change, and realized gross-margin bridges from 15-20 independent tax-preparation firms before and after workflow automation.
caveats: H&R Block's brand, scale, refund products, and national marketing may overstate independent LMM pricing power.
The estimate concerns retention of implemented gross benefit, not revenue growth, demand, or implementation success.
Self-prepared e-file volume was growing faster than professionally prepared e-file volume at the March 2026 snapshot, increasing competitive sharing risk (S12).

d5.bridge: IRS projects counts of returns processed, not a constant-price, constant-quality quantity index for services sold by 541213 firms. I use FY2026-to-FY2031 filing growth as the closest volume bridge, emphasizing individual returns because they dominate volumes while checking partnership and corporation returns. Counts may understate service demand if complexity per return rises and overstate it if data prefill or software reduces work content; channel displacement is reserved for o.
d5.rationale: IRS projects total individual income-tax returns rising from 164.135 million in FY2026 to 168.570 million in FY2031, a ratio of about 1.03; partnership returns rise from 5.200 million to 5.630 million, while corporation returns rise from 8.259 million to 8.463 million (S11). Statutory filing obligations make underlying quantity resilient, but the wide range allows policy simplification, economic formation changes, and shifts in return complexity.
d5.change_evidence: Build a return-type-weighted volume and complexity index from target-firm production data, IRS form/schedule counts, and realized revenue stripped of price changes, then update it with the next IRS Publication 6292 projection cycle.
caveats: IRS projections are national filing counts and do not isolate paid preparation or NAICS 541213.
The projection models extrapolate filing patterns and do not fully encode future tax-law simplification or major administrative redesign.
Return count is an imperfect measure of constant-quality service quantity because complexity varies materially across returns.

o.bridge: IRS channel statistics measure current professionally prepared versus self-prepared e-filed individual returns, not the 2031 share of the total service quantity requiring a 541213 operator. I start from the roughly one-half professional channel, shade the base downward for continued commercial self-service and AI assistance, and retain upside for complexity and accountability. Excluding paper returns and business returns can bias the starting share either way; equating 'professionally e-filed' with this narrower NAICS likely biases it upward because CPA firms also prepare returns.
o.rationale: In the March 2026 filing-season snapshot, tax professionals e-filed 39.738 million returns and self-preparers 37.836 million; professional volume was down 1.0% year over year while self-prepared volume was up 1.9% (S12). GAO states that more than half of individual taxpayers used paid preparers in FY2024 and notes that paid preparers must use PTINs and remain subject to tax-code requirements, supporting durable accountable-operator demand even as software handles more steps (S14). Current AI limitations on even simplified tax calculations also argue against rapid elimination of review (S4).
o.change_evidence: Obtain annual IRS paid-preparer flags by return complexity and preparer type, matched to customer migration cohorts among independent firms and controlled trials of end-to-end AI self-preparation through 2031.
caveats: The March 2026 data are an in-season snapshot and channel mix changes around deadlines and extensions.
IRS professional e-file statistics include CPA and other firms outside NAICS 541213.
An operator may remain legally or practically accountable while requiring far fewer labor hours; that labor effect belongs in a and rho, not o.

narrative.executive_view: Pure-play tax preparation is a repeat, externally purchased service with unusually document-heavy workflows and a statutory demand anchor. The opportunity is to automate intake, first-pass preparation, research, and review support while retaining a responsible preparer for exceptions and sign-off. The central tension is that the same technology can strengthen operator margins and make self-preparation or price competition easier.
narrative.ai_impact: AI can classify source documents, extract fields, reconcile records, draft client questions, retrieve tax authority, populate workpapers, and flag anomalies before review. O*NET shows that calculation, record examination, verification, research, and client explanation all sit inside the core job (S3). Adoption is accelerating (S5, S6), but TaxCalcBench's sub-one-third success rate on simplified returns makes governed integration with deterministic tax software and human review more credible than autonomous filing (S4).
narrative.value_capture: Because customers commonly buy a completed return at a form- or complexity-based price, fewer production hours do not mechanically reduce the invoice (S10). H&R Block's fiscal 2025 net average charge and assisted revenue growth show pricing can coexist with roughly flat volume (S9). Retention should still erode over time through renewal repricing, customer switching, free or low-cost software, and rivals adopting the same tools.
narrative.firm_availability: The six-digit industry definition is narrow and most LMM firms should fit the outsourced-service lens (S1), although owner dependence, franchise restrictions, compliance quality, and weak books can make a firm ineligible. Broad employer-owner intentions suggest roughly one qualifying transfer opportunity in five over five years, and observed accounting/tax practice sales confirm a functioning market, but neither source directly measures the frozen LMM population (S7, S8).
narrative.demand_durability: IRS forecasts imply slow growth in individual-return counts through 2031 and somewhat faster partnership-return growth (S11). Filing obligations and tax complexity support the underlying work basket, while current channel data still place professional and self-prepared e-file volumes near parity (S12). Operator-required demand is therefore durable but not protected: simple returns can migrate to software, while complex returns retain greater need for judgment and accountability.
narrative.risks_and_uncertainty: The largest evidence gaps are a six-digit wage-weighted task map, completed-transfer incidence for LMM tax firms, and longitudinal unit economics after automation. Survey adoption can be promotional, broad-industry staffing may misstate pure-play work, and preparer-channel statistics include CPA firms. Material downside would arise if integrated AI makes complex self-service reliable, price competition passes savings through quickly, or target firms prove too owner-dependent to transfer.
narrative.principal_driver: A large labor share sits in repeat document, calculation, research, and review workflows that can be compressed while customers continue buying a completed return.
narrative.principal_weakness: The technology that improves operator productivity can also shift simple returns to self-service and weaken long-run pricing retention.

sources.title: North American Industry Classification System: Sector 54, 541213 Tax Preparation Services
sources.supports: Defines 541213 as non-CPA establishments preparing tax returns without bookkeeping, billing, or payroll, and states that basic tax-law and filing knowledge is required.

sources.title: May 2023 National Industry-Specific Occupational Employment and Wage Estimates: NAICS 541200
sources.supports: Reports occupation shares and wages for the broader accounting, tax preparation, bookkeeping, and payroll industry, including accountants, tax preparers, bookkeeping clerks, and administrative occupations.

sources.title: O*NET OnLine: Tax Preparers 13-2082.00
sources.supports: Lists core tasks and work activities including tax computation, form completion, deduction selection, client interviews, record review, error checking, research, advice, and explanation of tax law.

sources.title: TaxCalcBench: Evaluating Frontier Models on the Tax Calculation Task
sources.supports: Finds state-of-the-art models correctly calculate fewer than one-third of federal returns in a simplified sample and documents tax-table, calculation, and eligibility errors.

sources.title: From Incubation to Integration: Generative AI Adoption Nearly Doubles as Professional Services Reach Crossroads
sources.supports: Reports tax-firm enterprise GenAI adoption rising from 8% to 21%, 79% expecting significant integration by 2027, and tax-return preparation as a use case among 63% of tax/accounting GenAI users.

sources.title: 7 insights for firm growth, efficiency and a competitive edge
sources.supports: Summarizes Intuit's April 2025 survey of 700 US accounting professionals, including 46% daily AI use, 81% reporting productivity gains, and stated automation patterns.

sources.title: Most Small-Business Owners Lack a Succession Plan
sources.supports: Reports that 52.3% of US employer businesses have owners age 55 or older and 22% of employer-business owners planned to sell or transfer ownership within five years.

sources.title: Accounting & Tax Practice Business Valuation Multiples & Financial Benchmarks
sources.supports: Documents a market for independent and franchise accounting/tax practices, reported sales during 2021-2025, a 2025 median sale price of $500,000, and transaction multiples.

sources.title: H&R Block, Inc. 2025 Form 10-K
sources.supports: Reports US assisted revenue up 6.1%, net average charge up 5.1%, company-owned return volume up 1.0%, total assisted volume down 0.9%, strong seasonality, and franchise/competitor acquisitions.

sources.title: Know the cost of tax preparation with H&R Block upfront pricing
sources.supports: Shows upfront per-engagement pricing based on customer circumstances, forms, states, and add-ons rather than an explicit cost-plus labor contract.

sources.title: IRS Publication 6292: Fiscal Year Return Projections for the United States, 2025-2032
sources.supports: Projects FY2026 and FY2031 return counts, including individual income-tax returns of 164.135 million and 168.570 million, partnerships of 5.200 million and 5.630 million, and corporations of 8.259 million and 8.463 million.

sources.title: Filing season statistics for week ending March 20, 2026
sources.supports: Reports 39.738 million professionally prepared e-file returns and 37.836 million self-prepared e-file returns, with year-over-year changes of negative 1.0% and positive 1.9%, respectively.

sources.title: Important Considerations as You Select Your Return Preparer This Filing Season
sources.supports: States that over 54% of individual income-tax returns were prepared by paid preparers and describes PTIN, due-diligence, credential, representation, and preparer-responsibility distinctions.

sources.title: Paid Tax Return Preparers: Opportunities Remain to Improve IRS Oversight
sources.supports: States that more than half of individual taxpayers used paid preparers in FY2024 and explains PTIN, tax-code, credential, error, and oversight requirements.
